philosophy: Every static question targets a known Algebra 1 misconception. DOK 2–3 emphasis — conceptual understanding, not just computation.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students call everything in an expression a 'variable'
questions.stem: In the expression 7x² − 3x + 5, which part is the constant term?
questions.explanation: A constant term has no variable. The constant here is 5. The coefficients are 7 and −3; x is the variable.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students apply commutativity to subtraction
questions.stem: Which property justifies rewriting 3(x + 5) as 3x + 15?
questions.explanation: The Distributive Property states a(b + c) = ab + ac. Here, 3 distributes over (x + 5).

questions.misconception_targeted: Students don't recognize when an identity or contradiction has been reached
questions.stem: Solve: 4(x + 2) = 4x + 9
options: C. No solution — the equation is a contradiction
D. All real numbers — the equation is always true
questions.explanation: Expanding: 4x + 8 = 4x + 9 → 8 = 9. This is always false — no solution.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students don't recognize an identity (infinite solutions) vs. one solution
questions.stem: Solve: 3(x − 2) = 3x − 6
options: D. Infinitely many solutions — true for all real x
questions.explanation: Expanding: 3x − 6 = 3x − 6 → −6 = −6. This is always true — infinite solutions.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students set up only one equation for absolute value, missing the negative case
questions.stem: Which correctly sets up the solution to |2x − 3| = 7?
questions.explanation: Absolute value equations split into two cases: the expression equals the positive value AND the expression equals the negative value.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students don't check for extraneous solutions
questions.stem: Solve |x + 4| = −2.
questions.explanation: Absolute value is always non-negative. No expression inside an absolute value can equal −2. There is no solution.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students forget to flip the inequality sign when multiplying/dividing by a negative
questions.stem: Solve: −3x > 12
questions.explanation: Dividing both sides by −3 REVERSES the inequality: x < −4. Failure to flip is the most common error in inequality solving.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students confuse AND (intersection) with OR (union) solution sets
questions.stem: What is the solution to: x + 1 < 4 AND x − 2 > −1?
options: A. x < 3 or x > 1 (all real numbers > 1)
questions.explanation: x < 3 AND x > 1 — AND means intersection: both must be true simultaneously. Solution: 1 < x < 3.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students think the same output for multiple inputs disqualifies a function
questions.stem: Which set of ordered pairs represents a function?
options: D. {(2,3), (2,4), (2,5)} — x=2 maps to three values
questions.explanation: A function requires each input to map to EXACTLY ONE output. In B, different inputs (3, 4, 5) each map to one output (5). Multiple inputs sharing an output is allowed.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students compute Δx/Δy instead of Δy/Δx
questions.stem: Find the slope of the line through (2, 5) and (6, 13).
questions.explanation: slope = (y₂ − y₁)/(x₂ − x₁) = (13 − 5)/(6 − 2) = 8/4 = 2. Reversing the fraction (Δx/Δy) gives 1/2 — the common error.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students confuse y = 3 (horizontal) with x = 3 (vertical)
questions.stem: Which equation represents a vertical line passing through (3, 7)?
questions.explanation: A vertical line has an undefined slope; its equation is x = constant. The point (3, 7) has x = 3, so x = 3 is the vertical line.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students think parallel lines must have the same y-intercept
questions.stem: Are the lines y = 3x + 5 and y = 3x − 2 parallel?
options: A. No — they have different y-intercepts
B. Yes — they have equal slopes and different y-intercepts
C. No — parallel lines must have the same y-intercept
D. Yes — they are the same line
questions.explanation: Parallel lines have equal slopes (both m = 3 here) and DIFFERENT y-intercepts. Same y-intercept would make them the same line.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students negate instead of taking negative reciprocal
questions.stem: A line has slope 2/3. What is the slope of a line perpendicular to it?
questions.explanation: Perpendicular slope = negative reciprocal. Reciprocal of 2/3 is 3/2; negate to get −3/2. Just negating (−2/3) is wrong — that's the additive inverse, not perpendicular.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students confuse slope and y-intercept in real-world context
questions.stem: A plumber charges $80 per hour plus a $50 service fee. In C = 80h + 50, what does the value 50 represent?
options: B. The flat service fee charged regardless of hours
D. The number of hours worked
questions.explanation: The y-intercept (50) is the value of C when h = 0 — the cost before any hours are worked, i.e., the flat service fee.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students think any point on either line is the solution
questions.stem: When solving a system of two linear equations graphically, the solution is:
options: A. Any point on the first line
B. Any point on the second line
C. The point(s) where both lines intersect — satisfying BOTH equations simultaneously
D. The y-intercept of both lines
questions.explanation: The solution to a system must satisfy ALL equations simultaneously — it is the intersection point.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students think parallel lines always have no solution but equal slopes always means same line
questions.stem: A system has equations y = 2x + 3 and y = 2x + 3. How many solutions does it have?
options: A. No solution — they are parallel
C. Infinitely many solutions — they are the same line
D. Two solutions — one for each equation
questions.explanation: Identical equations represent the SAME line — every point is a solution (infinitely many). Parallel lines have the SAME slope but DIFFERENT intercepts.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students multiply exponents when multiplying like bases (instead of adding)
questions.stem: Simplify x⁴ · x³.
questions.explanation: Product rule: same base, ADD exponents. x⁴ · x³ = x^(4+3) = x⁷. Multiplying (x¹²) applies the power-of-a-power rule wrongly.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students think b < 0 means decay, confusing sign of base with decay condition
questions.stem: Which condition on b determines whether y = a · bˣ represents exponential DECAY?
questions.explanation: Exponential decay requires the base b to be between 0 and 1 (exclusive). b > 1 gives growth. The value of a determines vertical stretch, not growth/decay.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students classify linear functions as exponential because they grow
questions.stem: Which table represents an EXPONENTIAL function?
questions.explanation: Exponential functions have a CONSTANT RATIO between consecutive y-values. In B, each y value is multiplied by 2. A and D have constant differences (linear); C has constant second differences (quadratic).

questions.misconception_targeted: Students don't distribute the negative sign to ALL terms when subtracting
questions.stem: Simplify: (5x² + 3x − 2) − (2x² − 4x + 1)
questions.explanation: Distribute the minus: −(2x² − 4x + 1) = −2x² + 4x − 1. Then: (5x² + 3x − 2) + (−2x² + 4x − 1) = 3x² + 7x − 3. The most common error is not flipping the sign of −4x to +4x.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students find factors that ADD to c and MULTIPLY to b (reversed)
questions.stem: Factor x² − 7x + 12.
questions.explanation: Find two numbers that MULTIPLY to 12 and ADD to −7: that's −3 and −4. So (x − 3)(x − 4). The reversed error (add to 12, multiply to −7) would give different numbers.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students try to factor a sum of squares (which is not factorable over the reals)
questions.stem: Which expression is a difference of two squares and can be factored?
questions.explanation: A difference of two squares: a² − b² = (a+b)(a−b). x² − 9 = x² − 3² = (x+3)(x−3). Note: x² + 9 is a SUM of squares — not factorable over the reals.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students apply wrong factoring method; jump to trinomial factoring without checking for GCF first
questions.stem: What is the BEST first step to factor 6x² − 24?
options: A. Look for two numbers that multiply to −24 and add to 0
D. Apply the difference of squares immediately to 6x² − 24
questions.explanation: Always check for a GCF first. GCF = 6: 6(x² − 4). Then x² − 4 is a difference of squares: 6(x+2)(x−2). Skipping the GCF step makes the remaining factoring harder.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students think any equation with x² is always in standard form
questions.stem: Which is a quadratic function in STANDARD FORM?
questions.explanation: Standard form is y = ax^2 + bx + c. Only B is in standard form. A and D are vertex form, C is factored form.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students confuse 'zeros' (x-intercepts) with y-intercept
questions.stem: A quadratic has zeros at x = 2 and x = −5. What do these zeros represent graphically?
options: A. The y-intercept of the parabola
C. The x-coordinates where the parabola crosses the x-axis
D. The value of a, b, or c in standard form
questions.explanation: Zeros are x-values where f(x) = 0 — meaning the graph crosses (or touches) the x-axis. They are NOT the y-intercept.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students substitute incorrectly into the quadratic formula — especially signs
questions.stem: For 2x² − 3x − 5 = 0, which correctly identifies a, b, and c for the quadratic formula?
questions.explanation: Standard form ax² + bx + c: here a = 2, b = −3 (the coefficient of x including its sign), c = −5 (the constant including its sign). Sign errors in b or c cause wrong discriminant and wrong solutions.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students misidentify function type from pattern in table
questions.stem: A table shows x: 0,1,2,3,4 and y: 1,2,4,8,16. The first differences are 1,2,4,8. What type of function is this?
options: B. Quadratic — the second differences might be constant
C. Exponential — the y-values have a constant ratio of 2
D. Cubic — it grows faster than linear
questions.explanation: Check consecutive ratios: 2/1=2, 4/2=2, 8/4=2, 16/8=2. Constant multiplicative ratio → exponential. The second differences (1,2,4,8) are NOT constant (that's quadratic).

questions.misconception_targeted: Students think standard deviation = range, or that it measures the maximum spread
questions.stem: Data Set A: {5, 5, 5, 5, 5} and Data Set B: {1, 3, 5, 7, 9} both have a mean of 5. Which has a larger standard deviation?
options: A. Data Set A — it has more values equal to the mean
B. Data Set B — its values are more spread out from the mean
C. They have equal standard deviation — same mean
D. Standard deviation cannot be compared across sets
questions.explanation: Standard deviation measures the average distance from the mean. All values in A equal the mean (SD = 0). Values in B vary from 4 units below to 4 units above the mean. B has a larger standard deviation.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students conclude causation from a strong correlation
questions.stem: A study finds a strong positive correlation (r = 0.95) between ice cream sales and drowning incidents. Which conclusion is valid?
options: A. Eating ice cream causes drowning
B. Drowning causes people to buy ice cream
C. Both are likely associated with a third variable (hot weather), but one does not cause the other
D. The data is invalid because the correlation is too high
questions.explanation: Correlation measures the strength of association — NOT causation. Both variables are likely driven by temperature (a lurking variable). This is a classic example of correlation ≠ causation.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students confuse slope and y-intercept meaning in regression context
questions.stem: A regression model predicts final exam score: y = 7.5x + 40, where x is hours studied. What does 40 represent?
options: A. The score improves by 40 points per hour of study
B. The predicted score for a student who studies 7.5 hours
C. The predicted score if a student studies 0 hours
questions.explanation: The y-intercept (40) is the predicted y when x = 0 — the model's predicted score with zero study hours. The slope (7.5) tells us how much the score increases per additional hour.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students don't know how to interpret a negative residual
questions.stem: A student studied 4 hours. The model predicts y = 70 but their actual score was 65. What is the residual and what does it mean?
options: A. Residual = +5; the model underestimated the score
B. Residual = −5; the model overestimated — actual score was below prediction
questions.explanation: Residual = actual − predicted = 65 − 70 = −5. A NEGATIVE residual means the actual value fell BELOW the prediction — the model overestimated.

questions.misconception_targeted: Students think r = 0 means no data trend rather than no LINEAR relationship
questions.stem: An r-value of 0 indicates:
options: A. A perfect positive linear relationship
B. A perfect negative linear relationship
C. No linear relationship (though a non-linear pattern may still exist)
D. The data has no pattern at all
questions.explanation: r = 0 means no LINEAR correlation. A perfect parabola has r ≈ 0 because it has no linear trend, yet it clearly follows a pattern.
